questions.question: What does BEM stand for?
questions.explanation: BEM stands for Block (the outer component), Element (a part of the block), and Modifier (a variation).

questions.question: Which BEM class name is correct for a 'title' element inside a 'card' block?
questions.explanation: BEM uses double underscore for elements: .block__element. .card__title means 'the title element of the card block'.

questions.question: What does the 'I' in ITCSS stand for?
questions.explanation: ITCSS = Inverted Triangle CSS. The triangle represents specificity and reach — broad at the top, specific at the bottom.

questions.question: Which CSS feature lets you explicitly control cascade layer order?
questions.explanation: @layer lets you define the order of cascade layers. Rules in earlier layers are overridden by later layers.

questions.question: What is the specificity of a single class selector like .card?
questions.explanation: A class selector has specificity 0,0,1,0. The four positions are: inline styles, IDs, classes/attributes/pseudo-classes, elements/pseudo-elements.

questions.question: Which OOCSS principle means a component's styles should not depend on where it is placed in the HTML?
questions.explanation: Separate container from content means a component should look the same regardless of where it's placed — no .sidebar .card styles.

questions.question: What is a design token?
options: A named CSS custom property representing a design decision (color, spacing, etc.)
A design pattern for CSS components
A naming convention for CSS classes
A token used in CSS preprocessors
questions.explanation: Design tokens are atomic design values stored as CSS custom properties — the single source of truth for colors, spacing, typography, etc.

questions.question: What is the recommended maximum specificity for maintainable CSS?
questions.explanation: For maintainable CSS, aim for 0,0,1,0 — a single class selector. This keeps specificity flat and easy to override.

questions.question: Which SMACSS prefix indicates a state class?
questions.explanation: SMACSS uses the .is- prefix for state classes like .is-active, .is-hidden, .is-disabled.

questions.question: What problem does CSS @layer solve?
options: It prevents specificity wars by explicitly defining the cascade order
It improves CSS performance by loading layers separately
It replaces the need for CSS preprocessors
It creates visual layers like Photoshop
questions.explanation: @layer solves the cascade/specificity problem by letting developers explicitly define which layer of styles takes priority.

questions.question: What is the ITCSS layer that comes first (lowest specificity)?
questions.explanation: Settings (variables/design tokens) comes first in ITCSS. These produce no actual CSS output but define the values used everywhere else.

questions.question: What is the ITCSS layer that comes last (highest specificity)?
questions.explanation: Utilities (overrides) come last in ITCSS. They have the highest specificity and override any component styles.

questions.question: Why should you avoid IDs in CSS selectors?
options: IDs have very high specificity (0,1,0,0) making them hard to override
IDs are slower for the browser to match
IDs are not valid CSS selectors in some browsers
IDs can only be used once per page, making them non-reusable
questions.explanation: ID selectors have 0,1,0,0 specificity — much higher than classes. Overriding them requires even more specific selectors or !important.

questions.question: What advantage does `:where(.card)` have over `.card`?
options: :where() has zero specificity, making it trivially easy to override
:where() is faster for the browser to parse
questions.explanation: :where() nullifies the specificity of its contents. `:where(.card)` has 0,0,0,0 specificity — the lowest possible.

questions.question: What does the SMACSS 'Layout' category typically use as a prefix?
questions.explanation: SMACSS uses .l- prefix for layout modules like .l-header, .l-sidebar, .l-main, .l-footer.

questions.question: In BEM, how do you write a modifier that applies to an element?
questions.explanation: Element modifiers use the pattern .block__element--modifier. For example: .card__title--large.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a CSS reset?
options: To normalize browser default styles for consistent cross-browser rendering
To remove all CSS from a page
To clear browser cache for stylesheets
questions.explanation: CSS resets (like normalize.css) remove or normalize inconsistent browser default styles so your CSS starts from a clean baseline.

questions.question: What is a benefit of using CSS custom properties over preprocessor variables?
options: Custom properties can be changed at runtime (media queries, JavaScript, themes)
Custom properties are faster to compile
Custom properties are supported in older browsers
questions.explanation: Unlike Sass/Less variables, CSS custom properties are live — they can be changed by media queries, JavaScript, or theme toggles at runtime.

questions.question: What is the correct @layer declaration order for lowest-to-highest priority?
questions.explanation: Layers are ordered lowest priority first. base is overridden by components, which is overridden by utilities.

questions.question: Which principle means 'separate structure from skin' in OOCSS?
options: Keep layout/position separate from visual design like colors and borders
Use different classes for different screen sizes
Don't use IDs in CSS selectors
questions.explanation: Structure (layout, positioning) should be separate from skin (colors, fonts, borders) so you can reuse the same layout with different visuals.
